diagnostics.title: await before an early-return guard
diagnostics.message: This await blocks the function before an early-return that doesn't use the awaited value, so the skip path waits for nothing. Move the await below the guard so it only runs when you need the data
diagnostics.help: Move the `await` below the early-return guard so the skip path stays fast and avoids unnecessary async work.

diagnostics.title: Request input spread without field allowlist
diagnostics.message: Request input is spread or merged into an object without a field allowlist, enabling mass assignment (client-set owner/role/price fields) or prototype pollution.
diagnostics.help: Assign explicit, allowlisted fields (or validate with a strict schema and no `.passthrough()`) instead of spreading/merging request input. Otherwise the client can set ownership, role, or price columns (mass assignment) or pollute the prototype.

diagnostics.title: Chained array iterations
diagnostics.message: This loops over your list twice because .map().filter() makes two passes, so do it in one pass with .reduce() or a for...of loop
diagnostics.help: Combine `.map().filter()` style chains into one pass with `.reduce()` or a `for...of` loop, so you only loop over the list once

diagnostics.help: Set an explicit button `type` so plain buttons do not submit forms by accident: `type="button"`, `"submit"`, or `"reset"`.

diagnostics.title: Interactive element not focusable
diagnostics.message: Keyboard users can't tab to this 'checkbox' because it isn't focusable, so add `tabIndex={0}`.
diagnostics.help: Add keyboard focus support so users can reach interactive elements without a pointer.

diagnostics.title: Click handler missing keyboard handler
diagnostics.message: Keyboard users can't trigger this click handler because there's no keyboard one, so add `onKeyUp`, `onKeyDown`, or `onKeyPress`.
diagnostics.help: Pair `onClick` with a key handler so keyboard users can trigger it.

diagnostics.title: Role used instead of HTML tag
diagnostics.message: Screen reader users get more reliable semantics from `<input>` than `role="checkbox"`, so use `<input>` instead.
diagnostics.help: Use the matching HTML element when one exists so browsers and assistive tech get native semantics.

diagnostics.title: Effect subscription or timer never cleaned up
diagnostics.message: `setTimeout` creates a timer in useEffect without returning cleanup. Return a cleanup function so it does not leak after unmount.
diagnostics.help: Return a cleanup function that stops the subscription or timer: `return () => target.removeEventListener(name, handler)` for listeners, `return () => clearInterval(id)` or `clearTimeout(id)` for timers, or `return unsubscribe` if the subscribe call already gave you one.

diagnostics.title: Multiple setState calls in one effect
diagnostics.message: 4 setState calls in one useEffect redraw your screen each time they run together.
diagnostics.help: Combine related updates in `useReducer` so one effect does not redraw the screen once per `setState` call.

diagnostics.title: Event logic handled in an effect
diagnostics.message: Faking an event handler with a prop plus a useEffect costs an extra render & runs late.
diagnostics.help: Run the side effect in the event handler that triggers it, instead of watching its state from a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#sharing-logic-between-event-handlers

diagnostics.title: Derived value copied into state
diagnostics.message: Storing "formData" in state when you can derive it from other values costs an extra render.
diagnostics.help: Work out the value while rendering (or with useMemo if it's expensive) instead of copying it into useState through a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#updating-state-based-on-props-or-state

diagnostics.title: State synced to a prop inside an effect
diagnostics.message: This effect adjusts state after a prop changes, so users briefly see the stale value.
diagnostics.help: Adjust the state inline during render with a `prev`-prop comparison (`if (prop !== prevProp) { setPrevProp(prop); setX(...); }`), or refactor to remove the duplicated state. Routing the adjustment through a useEffect forces an extra render with a stale UI between the two commits. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#adjusting-some-state-when-a-prop-changes

diagnostics.title: Custom modal instead of dialog

diagnostics.title: Custom modal instead of dialog
diagnostics.message: Keyboard users can tab out of this `role="dialog"` modal because it has no built-in focus trapping, so use the native `<dialog>`, which gives you focus trapping, `Escape` to close, and the backdrop for free.
diagnostics.help: Replace the wrapper with `<dialog>` and open it with `dialog.showModal()`. For the trigger, prefer `<button commandfor="id" command="show-modal">` (Chrome 135+), or a `useRef` with `dialogRef.current?.showModal()`.

diagnostics.title: Button missing explicit type
diagnostics.message: Your users can submit the form by accident because a `<button>` with no `type` defaults to submit.
diagnostics.help: Set an explicit button `type` so plain buttons do not submit forms by accident: `type="button"`, `"submit"`, or `"reset"`.

diagnostics.title: Component rendered by inline function call
diagnostics.message: "renderContent()" hides a component behind an inline call, so pull it into its own component and render it as JSX so React can track it.
diagnostics.help: Make it a named component rendered as JSX so React can track it and preserve its state.

diagnostics.title: Empty default prop breaks memo
diagnostics.message: This keeps redrawing children that compare props because default prop value [] makes a brand new array every render, so move it to a constant at the top of the file
diagnostics.help: Move it to the top of the file: `const EMPTY_ITEMS: Item[] = []`, then use that as the default value

diagnostics.message: Unused file is not reachable from any entry point, so it adds maintenance surface without shipping any code.
diagnostics.help: Delete the file if it is truly unreachable, or import it from an entry point.

diagnostics.title: Pure function rebuilt every render
diagnostics.message: `getRoleBadgeStyle` inside `UserMenu` uses no local state but is rebuilt on every render, so it wastes work & breaks memoized children. Move it to the top of the file, outside the component.
diagnostics.help: Move the function above the component, at the top of the file. It doesn't use local state, so rebuilding it each update is wasted work.

diagnostics.title: Effects chained together
diagnostics.message: Your screen redraws several times from a single action because one useEffect changes "localRedId", which sets off this one.
diagnostics.help: Compute as much as possible during render (e.g. `const isGameOver = round > 5`) and write all related state inside the event handler that originally fires the chain. Each effect link adds an extra render and makes the code rigid as requirements evolve

diagnostics.title: Derived state stored in an effect
diagnostics.message: You pay an extra render for state you can derive from other values.
diagnostics.help: Work out derived values while rendering: `const x = fn(dep)`. To reset a component's state when a prop changes, give it a key prop: `<Component key={prop} />`. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect

diagnostics.title: Prop mirrored into state via effect
diagnostics.message: Your screen shows the old value first because useState "localRedId" copies prop "selectedRedId" through this effect.
diagnostics.help: Delete both the `useState` and the `useEffect` and read the prop directly while rendering. Copying a prop into state shows the old value on the first render before the effect catches up.

diagnostics.title: Derived value copied into state
diagnostics.message: Storing "localRedId" in state when you can derive it from other values costs an extra render.
diagnostics.help: Work out the value while rendering (or with useMemo if it's expensive) instead of copying it into useState through a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#updating-state-based-on-props-or-state

diagnostics.title: Event logic handled in an effect
diagnostics.message: Faking an event handler with a prop plus a useEffect costs an extra render & runs late.
diagnostics.help: Run the side effect in the event handler that triggers it, instead of watching its state from a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#sharing-logic-between-event-handlers

diagnostics.title: Data passed to parent via effect
diagnostics.message: Handing data back to a parent from a useEffect costs your users an extra render.
diagnostics.help: Fetch the data in the parent and pass it down as a prop (or return it from the hook), instead of handing it back up through a prop callback in a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#passing-data-to-the-parent

diagnostics.title: Derived value copied into state
diagnostics.message: Storing "state" in state when you can derive it from other values costs an extra render.
diagnostics.help: Work out the value while rendering (or with useMemo if it's expensive) instead of copying it into useState through a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#updating-state-based-on-props-or-state

diagnostics.title: Chained array iterations
diagnostics.message: This loops over your list twice because .filter().map() makes two passes, so do it in one pass with .reduce() or a for...of loop
diagnostics.help: Combine `.map().filter()` style chains into one pass with `.reduce()` or a `for...of` loop, so you only loop over the list once

diagnostics.title: .map().filter(Boolean) loops twice
diagnostics.message: This loops over your list twice because .map().filter(Boolean) makes two passes, so use .flatMap() to change & drop items in one pass
diagnostics.help: Use `.flatMap(item => condition ? [value] : [])` to change and drop items in one pass, instead of building a throwaway array in between

diagnostics.title: State initialized from a mount effect
diagnostics.message: Your users see an extra render with empty "messages" because a useEffect sets its starting value.
diagnostics.help: Pass the initial value directly to useState() instead of setting it from a mount-only useEffect. For SSR hydration, prefer useSyncExternalStore().

diagnostics.help: These two awaits don't depend on each other. Wrap them in `Promise.all([...])` so they run at the same time.

diagnostics.title: await before an early-return guard
diagnostics.message: This await blocks the function before an early-return that doesn't use the awaited value, so the skip path waits for nothing. Move the await below the guard so it only runs when you need the data
diagnostics.help: Move the `await` below the early-return guard so the skip path stays fast and avoids unnecessary async work.

diagnostics.title: Chained array iterations
diagnostics.message: This loops over your list twice because .map().filter() makes two passes, so do it in one pass with .reduce() or a for...of loop
diagnostics.help: Combine `.map().filter()` style chains into one pass with `.reduce()` or a `for...of` loop, so you only loop over the list once

diagnostics.title: Spread copy before sort()
diagnostics.message: This wastes work because [...array].sort() copies the array just to sort it, so use array.toSorted() to sort without the extra copy (ES2023)
diagnostics.help: Use `array.toSorted()` (ES2023) instead of `[...array].sort()` so you sort without copying the array first

diagnostics.message: This makes the for…of loop slow because each await runs one after another, so collect the independent calls & run them together with `await Promise.all(items.map(...))`
diagnostics.help: Collect the items, then use `await Promise.all(items.map(...))` so independent work runs at the same time

diagnostics.help: Run the side effect in the event handler that triggers it, instead of watching its state from a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#sharing-logic-between-event-handlers

diagnostics.title: Chained array iterations
diagnostics.message: This loops over your list twice because .map().filter() makes two passes, so do it in one pass with .reduce() or a for...of loop
diagnostics.help: Combine `.map().filter()` style chains into one pass with `.reduce()` or a `for...of` loop, so you only loop over the list once

diagnostics.title: await inside a loop
diagnostics.message: This makes the for…of loop slow because each await runs one after another, so collect the independent calls & run them together with `await Promise.all(items.map(...))`
diagnostics.help: Collect the items, then use `await Promise.all(items.map(...))` so independent work runs at the same time

diagnostics.title: JSX element passed as a prop

diagnostics.help: Move the JSX outside the component or wrap it in `useMemo` so memoized children do not redraw every render.

diagnostics.message: Unused file is not reachable from any entry point, so it adds maintenance surface without shipping any code.

diagnostics.title: Heavy library loaded eagerly
diagnostics.message: "recharts" ships extra code to your users up front & slows page load. Load it on demand with React.lazy() or next/dynamic.
diagnostics.help: Load it only when needed: `const Component = dynamic(() => import('library'), { ssr: false })` from next/dynamic, or React.lazy().

diagnostics.title: Static value rebuilt every render

diagnostics.help: Move the value above the component, at the top of the file. It doesn't use local state, so rebuilding it each update is wasted and makes it look new every time.

diagnostics.help: Move the JSX outside the component or wrap it in `useMemo` so memoized children do not redraw every render.

diagnostics.title: Effect subscription or timer never cleaned up
diagnostics.message: `setTimeout` creates a timer in useEffect without returning cleanup. Return a cleanup function so it does not leak after unmount.
diagnostics.help: Return a cleanup function that stops the subscription or timer: `return () => target.removeEventListener(name, handler)` for listeners, `return () => clearInterval(id)` or `clearTimeout(id)` for timers, or `return unsubscribe` if the subscribe call already gave you one.

diagnostics.help: Combine related updates in `useReducer` so one effect does not redraw the screen once per `setState` call.

diagnostics.title: Derived value copied into state

diagnostics.title: State synced to a prop inside an effect
diagnostics.message: This effect adjusts state after a prop changes, so users briefly see the stale value.
diagnostics.help: Adjust the state inline during render with a `prev`-prop comparison (`if (prop !== prevProp) { setPrevProp(prop); setX(...); }`), or refactor to remove the duplicated state. Routing the adjustment through a useEffect forces an extra render with a stale UI between the two commits. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#adjusting-some-state-when-a-prop-changes

diagnostics.title: State synced to a prop inside an effect

diagnostics.help: Compute as much as possible during render (e.g. `const isGameOver = round > 5`) and write all related state inside the event handler that originally fires the chain. Each effect link adds an extra render and makes the code rigid as requirements evolve

diagnostics.title: Effects chained together

diagnostics.help: Compute as much as possible during render (e.g. `const isGameOver = round > 5`) and write all related state inside the event handler that originally fires the chain. Each effect link adds an extra render and makes the code rigid as requirements evolve

diagnostics.title: Derived state stored in an effect
diagnostics.message: You pay an extra render for state you can derive from other values.
diagnostics.help: Work out derived values while rendering: `const x = fn(dep)`. To reset a component's state when a prop changes, give it a key prop: `<Component key={prop} />`. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect

diagnostics.title: Prop mirrored into state via effect
diagnostics.message: Your screen shows the old value first because useState "localRedId" copies prop "selectedRedId" through this effect.
diagnostics.help: Delete both the `useState` and the `useEffect` and read the prop directly while rendering. Copying a prop into state shows the old value on the first render before the effect catches up.

diagnostics.title: Derived value copied into state
diagnostics.message: Storing "localRedId" in state when you can derive it from other values costs an extra render.
diagnostics.help: Work out the value while rendering (or with useMemo if it's expensive) instead of copying it into useState through a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#updating-state-based-on-props-or-state

diagnostics.title: Pure function rebuilt every render
diagnostics.message: `getSelectClassName` inside `CascadingSelector` uses no local state but is rebuilt on every render, so it wastes work & breaks memoized children. Move it to the top of the file, outside the component.
diagnostics.help: Move the function above the component, at the top of the file. It doesn't use local state, so rebuilding it each update is wasted work.

diagnostics.title: State initialized from a mount effect
diagnostics.message: Your users see an extra render with empty "mounted" because a useEffect sets its starting value.
diagnostics.help: Pass the initial value directly to useState() instead of setting it from a mount-only useEffect. For SSR hydration, prefer useSyncExternalStore().

diagnostics.suppressionHint: oxlint matches plugin rules only by their full name, so `react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` in your eslint-disable comment does not silence `react-doctor/exhaustive-deps` — add `react-doctor/exhaustive-deps` to the comment (e.g. `react-hooks/exhaustive-deps, react-doctor/exhaustive-deps`).

diagnostics.title: Event logic handled in an effect
diagnostics.message: Faking an event handler with a prop plus a useEffect costs an extra render & runs late.
diagnostics.help: Run the side effect in the event handler that triggers it, instead of watching its state from a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#sharing-logic-between-event-handlers

diagnostics.title: Data passed to parent via effect
diagnostics.message: Handing data back to a parent from a useEffect costs your users an extra render.
diagnostics.help: Fetch the data in the parent and pass it down as a prop (or return it from the hook), instead of handing it back up through a prop callback in a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#passing-data-to-the-parent

diagnostics.title: Derived value copied into state
diagnostics.message: Storing "state" in state when you can derive it from other values costs an extra render.
diagnostics.help: Work out the value while rendering (or with useMemo if it's expensive) instead of copying it into useState through a useEffect. See https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect#updating-state-based-on-props-or-state

diagnostics.title: Chained array iterations
diagnostics.message: This loops over your list twice because .filter().map() makes two passes, so do it in one pass with .reduce() or a for...of loop
diagnostics.help: Combine `.map().filter()` style chains into one pass with `.reduce()` or a `for...of` loop, so you only loop over the list once

diagnostics.title: .map().filter(Boolean) loops twice
diagnostics.message: This loops over your list twice because .map().filter(Boolean) makes two passes, so use .flatMap() to change & drop items in one pass
diagnostics.help: Use `.flatMap(item => condition ? [value] : [])` to change and drop items in one pass, instead of building a throwaway array in between

diagnostics.title: Chained array iterations

diagnostics.help: Combine `.map().filter()` style chains into one pass with `.reduce()` or a `for...of` loop, so you only loop over the list once

diagnostics.title: State initialized from a mount effect
diagnostics.message: Your users see an extra render with empty "messages" because a useEffect sets its starting value.
diagnostics.help: Pass the initial value directly to useState() instead of setting it from a mount-only useEffect. For SSR hydration, prefer useSyncExternalStore().

diagnostics.title: await inside a loop
diagnostics.message: This makes the for…of loop slow because each await runs one after another, so collect the independent calls & run them together with `await Promise.all(items.map(...))`
diagnostics.help: Collect the items, then use `await Promise.all(items.map(...))` so independent work runs at the same time
